source: Anemia (low hematocrit) is a common finding in rheumatoid arthritis due to chronic inflammation and iron deficiency.
Anemia (low hemoglobin) is a common finding in rheumatoid arthritis due to chronic inflammation and iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) suggests microcytic anemia, which can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis due to iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) suggests microcytic anemia, which can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis due to iron deficiency.
Low mean corpuscular volume (MCV) suggests microcytic anemia, which can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis due to iron deficiency.
Elevated platelet count (PLT) can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis due to inflammation and thrombocytosis.
Low red blood cell count (RBC) suggests anemia, which can be seen in rheumatoid arthritis due to chronic inflammation and iron deficiency.
Elevated red cell distribution width (RDW) suggests increased variation in red blood cell size, which can be seen in early inflammatory processes like rheumatoid arthritis.
Elevated white blood cell count (WBC) can indicate inflammation and infection, which may be associated with rheumatoid arthritis.
